narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on their academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 32.50, Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 3, full-time enrollment plans, and family income—and predicted a 55% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This probability crossed the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, actually pushing the prediction toward bar passage despite being below the typical 3.21 average, suggesting it was a relative bright spot in your profile. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the average of about 5.5—had the next biggest impact, nudging the model toward expecting failure by signaling weaker performance relative to peers that year. Your upper-middle family income also contributed notably toward the failure prediction, as the model associates certain income brackets with varying bar success patterns compared to the dataset's average. Your LSAT score, while below the 36.7 average, and lower Year 1 decile played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Overall, your academic deciles and family background tipped the scales more heavily than your GPA or LSAT, placing you below typical admitted students who tend to have higher decile rankings and GPAs aligning closer to or above averages. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal story, but rather how these metrics stacked up statistically. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic performance through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and unique perspective as a Black man pursuing law. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and gain admission elsewhere—reach out to admissions counselors for targeted advice, and keep pursuing your goals with persistence.
